name: Fu Lu Shou
description: Fu Lu Shou are the collective representation of three star gods (or stellar deities) in Chinese folk religion. They embody the three essential aspirations of a good life: Fu (福) represents happiness and blessings, often associated with prosperity and good fortune; Lu (祿) symbolizes status, influence, and official salary; and Shou (壽) embodies longevity and a long life. They are rarely worshipped individually but are venerated as a group, embodying a holistic ideal of well-being. Their images, often depicted as elderly men with distinctive attributes, are common in Chinese households and businesses, intended to bring good luck and ward off misfortune. Fu Lu Shou are deeply ingrained in Chinese culture, representing the aspirations of a prosperous, successful, and long life. The integration of these three deities into a single concept highlights the Chinese emphasis on a balanced and harmonious existence, where material wealth, social standing, and health are equally valued.
subtitle: The Three Star Gods
titles: The Star Gods of Fortune, Prosperity, and Longevity

keyMyths.title: Fu and the Mandarin's Daughter
keyMyths.description: A popular myth tells of Fu, who was a righteous mandarin. He risked his life to save a village from bandits and was rewarded with immense fortune and happiness. This myth emphasizes the importance of moral virtue and the idea that good deeds lead to prosperity and blessings. His actions demonstrated a commitment to his community, earning him the favor of the heavens.
keyMyths.source: Werner, E.T.C. Myths and Legends of China. George G. Harrap & Co., 1922.

keyMyths.title: Lu and the Imperial Examinations
keyMyths.description: Lu is often associated with success in the Imperial Examinations. It is said that those who prayed to Lu would be granted high ranks and official positions. This myth highlights the importance of education and meritocracy in Chinese society. Lu represents the ambition and aspiration for social advancement through hard work and dedication to scholarship, leading to a life of influence.
keyMyths.source: Williams, C.A.S. Chinese Symbolism and Art Motives. Charles E. Tuttle Co., 1974.

keyMyths.title: Shou and the Peach of Immortality
keyMyths.description: Shou is frequently depicted holding a peach, which symbolizes immortality. Legend has it that he acquired this peach from the Queen Mother of the West, Xi Wangmu, granting him a long and healthy life. This myth underscores the Chinese desire for longevity and the belief in the possibility of extending one's lifespan through various means, including healthy living and divine favor. The peach remains a powerful symbol of long life.
keyMyths.source: Mackenzie, Donald A. Myths of China and Japan. The Gresham Publishing Company, 1923.

sources.author: Werner, E.T.C.
